speaker: interviewer
text: October 11, 1995. Survivor is Israel Berkenwald, and this is tape four. Towards the end of '44, middle of '44, the transports were going, and did you end up on one?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in 1944, in July, one of the important factories in the ghetto all of a sudden closed. And they started taking out the machinery, fabric, and even unfinished work was taken-- taken out from that factory, carted onto trucks, and taken out of the ghetto. That was a very bad sign. And the sign was that something is going on. Since they picked on their first factory, an important factory in the ghetto, that made uniforms for women in the German army, is being closed, God knows which factory is going to be closed next. So we knew something was going on.

speaker: interviewer
text: We also knew that the Warsaw ghetto had a uprising. And the Warsaw ghetto is no more. Therefore, we were afraid, what is actually going on, that they really picked on this factory to be closed?

speaker: interviewee
text: And we started hearing rumors. Because you actually been fed rumors since the first day of the war-- we were fed rumors. And then another factory was closed, a men's factory was closed. And then we walked out in the morning and we saw a sign, which was hung out-- every couple of days we had a sign on the walls to read. This is happening, or the rations are changed. This case was to the inhabitants of ghetto Lodz-- the ghetto is being resettled to a different part of Germany. And every able man, woman, and child that is able to work and contribute to the betterment of the German land, and help the German army that is fighting a successful war against the Americans, and against the English, and against the Russians, will be resettled into a different part of Germany, and work, and be fed, and be clothed-- and not to worry about the future. And the resettlement will start as of September 15 or 16-- or September 1st, I think, they're going to start the resettlement. And everybody that is being resettled will be notified. And his place of work, he'll be notified.

speaker: interviewee
text: So naturally, the first factory, everybody who worked in that factory was notified to report to the rail station so and so. He could take with him 40 pounds of luggage, clothes-- That's quite a lot, 40.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that includes whatever you could carry. Actually, whatever you could carry. 40 pounds is really not so much-- 40 pounds is a little bit under 20 kilos.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, am I right? No, it's not 20 kilo a pound-- I have to figure it out. Well, I know from air travel, it's a large-- it's a comfortable-sized suitcase.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, well--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Actually, it's more than that, because if a parent had a young child, yeah, the young child was entitled to some luggage.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was the first resettlement, sort of. And these people had to report at the train station at 9 o'clock in the morning. As I said, there was a train station in the ghetto that was made-- it was never there. But they made a track into the ghetto with a train station.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the people had to report to the train. And we sort of started suspecting this is the beginning of the end. Because we did hear rumors-- we always heard rumors, because we always had-- as little contact as we had with the outside world, we did have. I had a friend by the name of [NON-ENGLISH] that had a radio in the ghetto. He was found out, was taken into the kripo and shot. And there were more radios, so we did hear some-- some rumors. Some of them, we just listened to and forgot about it. Some of them, somehow, made sense. Some trains that went to Auschwitz and came back had scratches on the walls-- that we realized what it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Messages scratched into the walls?

speaker: interviewee
text: Messages, yes. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But we still didn't believe it. Eventually, it came to the part of the ghetto that I lived. And the announcement was that this part of the ghetto is being closed completely. Whoever will be found in about 48 hours in that part of the ghetto will be shot on the spot. Well, that meant that one third of the ghetto is going away.

speaker: interviewee
text: So instead of reporting to the train, we were hiding for a few days in the other part of the ghetto. Now the other part of the ghetto was being closed. And there's no place to hide. Finally, my two sisters and their husbands decided, come with me, let's report to the train. And let's go where they tell us. What could happen to us? What could happen to us?

speaker: interviewee
text: And we reported, willingly. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Not with a great feeling, but reported. We were loaded on the train. We were given, I think, a half of bread we were given each. Only those who reported-- those who were caught did not get any. And we were packed in like sardines into a train. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] There was actually standing room. There was very little room that you could lay down, with the exception of the people in transport that died. We had to put them into a corner, one on top of the other. If you had to go out for your need, you couldn't go out because the train was shut, the door was shut with a lock. But there was a barrel in the middle of the room. No privacy-- men, women, children, no privacy. That's where you were able to relieve yourself, which, of course, was no joy. We traveled a half a day and a night, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] under terrible conditions in the train. Terrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it very hot at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hot? Unbearably hot. You must realize, it's September. It's still hot. Although, in our part of Poland, September is not so hot anymore. But we were going down south, towards Krakow. Auschwitz is west of Krakow-- west or south-- southwest of Krakow. We arrived early in the morning. It wasn't light yet. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The train stopped, and the getting off the train [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] was hell on Earth. First of all, you had prisoners that were there before. They were wearing striped uniforms with striped hats. And they tell us, out! Out! Out! If you don't go out faster, you'll get hit, you'll get killed. Out! Out! Everybody out, everybody out!

speaker: interviewee
text: So we walked out of the train-- it was a cattle train, of course, it wasn't a passenger train. It was a cattle train. And we were packed like cattle.

speaker: interviewee
text: We got off the train amid shots-- there was shooting. Dogs were jumping and barking. The German was screaming with the most profane language imaginable. And they held in their hand those leather straps that you use when you ride a horse, hitting and beating without any pity. Then they'd tell-- men separate, women separate, men separate, women separate, men separate, women separate. And so we listened. The men go separately, the women go separately. And then we formed a line, walking towards the end. And the women were also walking towards the end. And a German officer, whether it was Mengele or not, I don't know-- we were walking towards him, and he held that leather strap in his hand, and it was folded in half. Right, left, right, left, left, right. Now it was quite obvious at that time that the people that were going right were people like me. They still looked like a human being. They still looked like they're able to work. But the ones that were going to left were older people, were people that looked like time has passed them by-- people that, on their size, they should weigh at least 175 pounds. They weighed 100 pounds. So we knew that these people are going someplace that is not right. And of-- of course, the same thing happened in the women lines. Only in the women lines was a bigger tragedy, because the women that had small children with them went immediately left. And if they have a small kid on them, and a 12- or 13-year-old daughter, and she went right, a fight broke out right away.

speaker: interviewee
text: The German said, you want to go with your mother? Go with your mother. And that's exactly what happened in these lines.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, I had a cousin that was with my sister, with my older sister. My older sister was an exceptionally beautiful woman. She was with my sister, and my sister was picked out from the line and put on the side of about seven or eight other women that also looked very good, and they were very beautiful-- beautiful women.

speaker: interviewer
text: You saw this?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I was on the side of the men. But when we came into camp, we saw that some people were hanging on the wires, and they looked black like coal. We didn't know that those were electric wires. Some people did. Some people in the camp just in order to end it all, threw themselves on the wires, and that was the easiest way to die. You got an electric shock, you're electrocuted and you're dead. These people knew. We didn't. That is how my sister died. And she gave word to us in camp what had happened. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My sister committed suicide the day she arrived in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is Sara?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sara, the oldest, yes. She was 18 years older than  me. And at the age of 38, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] she looked beautiful. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] In Auschwitz, I was put into a block. Some people were lucky-- they went into blocks, barracks that had wood where you could sleep, under first, or second, or third. My barrack was not finished yet, so we slept on the hard floor-- cold, hard floor.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't tell me exactly what happened when you arrived, when you went in. Did you go in a shower or anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well--

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, if you don't remember--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I remember everything-- yeah, I remember it very well. We went in a very large field when we arrived, and we were finally picked. We went into a very large field. And we stood in that field, and a prisoner-- a Jewish prisoner-- came up and he stood on a small platform and gave us a speech. Dear Jews, you came here in a camp, and you'll live. You'll only live if you give anything that you have hidden in your body. Because we know-- and it's true, people had hidden things in their body, whether it was a ring, a diamond, a watch, or money, or gold pieces.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you say in the body, you mean in the anus?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, in the anus, or in the mouth, or if you swallowed something. If the Germans will find it, if the German will know that you have something, you're shot on the spot. You better give everything out now, because you're going to shower, and you're going to get a uniform, and you look like everyone else here. But give all your possessions up right now, and that will save your life.

speaker: interviewee
text: So whoever wanted, gave everything up. We should have given it up anyway, because then we went into another room where we had to undress completely-- completely, with the exception of the shoes. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Then we had to go into another room, where you had a lot of prisoners sitting on benches and you were shaved-- head, armpits, and your private parts, completely and totally shaved. Then you went through another room of people that were sitting in benches, and wiped your body where you were shaved with some kind of chemical that was burning your body like fire. Now when you saw from far away that you're going into the shower, you couldn't wait to get into the shower. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We went, we showered, we walked through the other end, we got a striped uniform. The women didn't have striped uniforms yet. They were getting-- giving out clothes, because the barracks, the fences, you could see how the women were dressed. But I was-- in-- in-- in that transport, they still have the striped uniforms with striped hats-- no underwear, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] no socks, just wooden shoes-- they called them Trippen-- wooden shoes, a striped uniform, and a striped hat.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about a number?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did not get a number because in 1944, in September and October, that was a lucky time to come in to Auschwitz, believe it or not. That was the order that came from the high command in Germany to every camp-- don't kill people if they're able-bodied, because we need them now. And that was the time when they started drafting people up to 65 years old, in boys, 12 years and up, to go into the landover's army, German landover's army, or to the other army. Because they received such a beating in the Soviet Union, they didn't have anyone to save them, and it was already after VE Day. So they were being beaten in the west, and they lost the east.

speaker: interviewer
text: But what was happening to you in the camp at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: So in Auschwitz, I was there for about eight to nine days. And the-- the man that was in charge of the barracks said that everyone that came here must tell him and he will register you-- what you are, whether you're a tailor, whether you are a bricklayer, whether you are a shoemaker-- whatever your profession is, whatever your trade is. I had a very dear friend-- I still have him. He's also in Israel, now. He was a bricklayer. And I didn't want to part with him. And his father was a bricklayer.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he says, you know, why don't you tell him that you're a bricklayer, so maybe we'll go together. Which, I did and saved my life. So I became a bricklayer.

speaker: interviewee
text: And about a week or eight days later, we were put in a transport going to Braunschweig, Germany. And we didn't get numbers. We did get numbers on the uniforms. We got numbers-- my number was 50,554. And we went into the train, from Auschwitz to the train, and went all the way to Braunschweig.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long?

speaker: interviewee
text: Braunschweig is a town near Hanover. And in Braunschweig, the part of the town called [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH] were two camps, empty camps. And there was only one barrack. And we were 150 in the transport. And we were assigned to build barracks for new prisoners that will be coming. And I became a bricklayer. And I was doing very well as a bricklayer. I learned a trade and I was good at it. After we finished the barracks, which took, of course, a nice couple of weeks, I was going out to the factory, and I was building platforms for heavy machinery, which is, really, it's an hour to do it. You take four two-by-twos, you make a square, and then you pour cement in it. And the engineer that watched over us put in screws into the cement to stand up. So it was already, with the measurement for the machinery that is going to go on this platform. And that's what I did after we finish building the barracks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that also at Braunschweig?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in Braunschweig, [NON-ENGLISH]. That was two small villages. But it was Braunschweig. And then the war started getting closer and closer from the west, and from the north, and from the east, so-- and now, I'm rushing you through a little bit. They closed Braunschweig, and they sent us to a town called Watenstedt. Watenstedt had a munition factory called the Hermann Goering Werke. The factory belonged to Hermann Goering. And over there, we were making straps for bullets for machine guns. And over there, we were just a short time, about three, four weeks, or maybe even less. And the English army started getting closer. From there, we went to Helmstedt.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what month did you arrive at Helmstedt? And think back-- can you just put months on this? Braunschweig, I believe, was up to the--

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, yesterday, I remembered the exact-- Braunschweig was October. And Braunschweig was four months. Then we went to Watenstedt for about three to four weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was as far as January.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, then in Helmstedt and Bendorf together, we were about a month. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Then we were in a small camp. We were constantly on the go-- sometimes by foot, and sometimes by open car-- cattle trains.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do in Helmstedt and then in Bendorf?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, for the munition factory. Helmstedt, Bendorf, we were making the V1 and V2 caps for those bombs that were flying into London. They made such chaos in London.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in Bendorf?

speaker: interviewee
text: Helmstedt and Bendorf was right near to each other. It was salt mines, but they were connected. Then from there, we went to Wobbelin just for a couple of days-- around five, six days. And the English army was nearby. They took us out and we walked from there into a train. We were on the train for 10 days. Then they unloaded us.

speaker: interviewer
text: 10 Days, tell me about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: These 10 days were terrible. You had cannibalism going on on the train. There was no food or water. And I was put in a train with dead people-- and that's what save my life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is that what saved your life, too?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's what saved my life. The dead wagon saved my life, because when they opened up the train to go into Ludwigslust, I just walked off the train. And my friend, that lived right here on this block, almost dropped dead seeing me being alive, because he helped take me off the train to put me in there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they eat the liver?

speaker: interviewee
text: They ate anything they could get a hold of. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Ludwigslust was also cannibalism going on. And one morning, we wake up, and the camp is quiet. We don't hear dogs. We don't hear screams. We don't hear rifles. And we don't know what's going on. It was real weird. And then a Jeep came over, and the four soldiers that came off the Jeep didn't look like Germans. They looked different. And on the shoulder, they had an emblem-- airborne. It was the 82nd Airborne Division of the American Army, that parachuted that night in Ludwigslust. And we were liberated by the American Army. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Now what do we do-- what do you do with freedom? What do you do with freedom? It wasn't quite the end of the war yet, was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was six days before the end of the war. The war ended the 8th. I was liberated on the 2nd, 10 o'clock in the morning. I was in that camp for a couple of days because they couldn't transfer us any place. And then they transferred us to a German army barracks, where they kept horses, and that's where we were for a couple of days. And I decided I don't want to live in barrack anymore. Come what may-- I'm going back to Poland. Going back to Poland is another life story. I went back to Poland, the trains were loaded all the way up.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before you get on to that, could you tell me something of the help you received after you were liberated?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when we leave-- actually, what we received was actually K-rations. K-rations.

speaker: interviewer
text: No medical attention?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I actually didn't need medical attention. Maybe some people that needed medical attention went to the hospital, the field hospitals. I didn't need any medical attention. I didn't. And if I did need it, I wouldn't even tell them because I was trained five and a half years-- whatever you do, whatever you say, don't do or say that you're sick. Sick was a death sentence.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before you leave this period, tell me how you think you survived all this? What sustained you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Dumb luck. Dumb luck.

speaker: interviewer
text: Not a strength within you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think I had any strength with me, because--

speaker: interviewer
text: In you.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I was on that wagon, I asked my friend Harry Markowitz, who was my next door-- a few houses away from here. We are still very close. I said, please take me off the wagon, put me among the dead, because otherwise they're going to eat me alive. And I have no strength, or life in me to fight it over. And he did it.

speaker: interviewer
text: He did what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Put me into the wagon with the dead people. And that's how I lived through.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did being in the wagon with the dead--

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I didn't have people that are alive and strong enough to cut my leg off, or cut my buttock off, or cut my arm off.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean they were eating people who were not dead yet?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: I thought they only ate the--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, dead carcasses? No, no, no, no. Why should they eat the dead when the could eat alive?

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, ask the other way around. Why should they eat the live when they could eat the dead?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I guess the dead have no blood in them, have no warmth in them, where the live ones still have warmth. I mean, there's a lot of explanations that you could give. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So, what I decided-- what I decided right after the war, I'll go back home. I don't believe that everybody is dead.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll go on on the next tape.
